subject: Mathematics
q:
If the system of linear equations,
x+y+z=6
x+2y+3z=10
3x+2y+λz=μ
has more than two solutions, then μ-λ2, is equal to.
question:
If the system of linear equations,
x+y+z=6
x+2y+3z=10
3x+2y+λz=μ
has more than two solutions, then μ-λ2, is equal to.
solution:
x+y+z=6  ...1
x+2y+3z=10  ...2
3x+2y+λz=μ  …3

From 1 & 2,
If z=0⇒x+y=6 and x+2y=10, ⇒y=4, x=2
If y=0⇒x+z=6 and x+3z=10, ⇒z=2, x=4

So, 3x+2y+λz=μ, must pass through (2,4,0) and (4,0,2).

6+8=μ⇒μ=14
And 12+2λ=μ ⇒λ=1

So, μ-λ2=14-1=13

subject: Mathematics
q:
If the system of equations
$\begin{aligned}
& 2 x+7 y+\lambda z=3 \\
& 3 x+2 y+5 z=4 \\
& x+\mu y+32 z=-1
\end{aligned}$
has infinitely many solutions, then $(\lambda-\mu)$ is equal to________
question:
If the system of equations
$\begin{aligned}
& 2 x+7 y+\lambda z=3 \\
& 3 x+2 y+5 z=4 \\
& x+\mu y+32 z=-1
\end{aligned}$
has infinitely many solutions, then $(\lambda-\mu)$ is equal to________
solution: $\begin{aligned} & \mathrm{D}=\mathrm{D}_1=\mathrm{D}_2=\mathrm{D}_3=0 \\ & \mathrm{D}_3=\left|\begin{array}{ccc}2 & 7 & 3 \\ 3 & 2 & 4 \\ 1 & \mu & -1\end{array}\right|=0 \Rightarrow \mu=-39 \\ & \mathrm{D}=\left|\begin{array}{ccc}2 & 7 & \lambda \\ 3 & 2 & 5 \\ 1 & -39 & 32\end{array}\right|=0 \Rightarrow \lambda=-1 \\ & \lambda-\mu=38\end{aligned}$

solution:
For infinite number of solutions, all determinants D, D1, D2 and D3 must be zero and planes represented by these equations should not be parallel. So, we have,

D=11112313λ=0 ⇒λ=5

D1=211523μ3λ=0

D2=1211531μλ=0

And D3=11212513μ=0 ⇒μ=8

solution:
Given,

System of linear equations,

x+2y+3z=4

x+2y+3z=4

Now finding,

△=242a1232-52

⇒△=-18a-3

Hence, for a=3, △=0

Now finding,

△3=b2441282-5

⇒△3=9b-8

So, for b=8, △3=0

Now solving options we get,

If a=3 and b=8 we'll have infinite solution

For a=8,b=8, △≠0, △3=0⇒unique solution

For a=b=6, △≠0, △3≠0⇒ unique solution

∴ For a=3,b=6 we'll have no solution

subject: Mathematics
q:
Let $A$ and $B$ be two invertible matrices of order $3 \times 3$. If $\operatorname{det}\left(\mathrm{ABA}^{\mathrm{T}}\right)=8$ and det $\left(\mathrm{AB}^{-1}\right)=8$, then det $\left(\mathrm{BA}^{-1} \mathrm{~B}^{\mathrm{T}}\right)$ is

equal to
question:
Let $A$ and $B$ be two invertible matrices of order $3 \times 3$. If $\operatorname{det}\left(\mathrm{ABA}^{\mathrm{T}}\right)=8$ and det $\left(\mathrm{AB}^{-1}\right)=8$, then det $\left(\mathrm{BA}^{-1} \mathrm{~B}^{\mathrm{T}}\right)$ is

equal to
solution:
Let $|A|=a,|B|=b$

$\left.\Rightarrow \quad \mid A^{\eta}\right\rceil=a\left|A^{-1}\right|=\frac{1}{a},\left|B^{\eta}\right|=b,\left|B^{-1}\right|=\frac{1}{b}$

$\because \quad A B A^{\eta}=8 \Rightarrow|A||B| \mid A^{\eta}-8 \ldots(1)$

$\Rightarrow \quad a \cdot b \cdot a=8 \Rightarrow a^{2} b=8$

$\because \quad A B^{-1}|=8 \Rightarrow H|\left|B^{-1}\right|=8 \Rightarrow a, \frac{1}{b}=8$

From (1) \& (2)

$a=4, b=\frac{1}{2}$

Then, $\left|B A^{-1} B^{\eta}\right|=|B|\left|A^{-1}\right|\left|B^{\eta}\right|=b \cdot \frac{1}{a} \cdot b=\frac{b^{2}}{a}=\frac{1}{16}$

question:

1+cos2⁡θx+sin2⁡θy+4sin⁡3θz=0

cos2⁡θx+1+sin2⁡θy+4sin⁡3θz=0

cos2⁡θx+sin2⁡θy+(1+4sin⁡3θ)z=0
has a non-trivial solution, then the value of θ is:
solution:

subject: Mathematics
q:
If the system of equations
$$
\begin{aligned}
& x+y+z=6 \\
& x+2 y+3 z=10 \\
& x+2 y+\lambda z=0
\end{aligned}
$$
has a unique solution, then $\lambda$ is not equal to
question:
If the system of equations
$$
\begin{aligned}
& x+y+z=6 \\
& x+2 y+3 z=10 \\
& x+2 y+\lambda z=0
\end{aligned}
$$
has a unique solution, then $\lambda$ is not equal to
solution:
Given system of equations is
$$
\begin{aligned}
& x+y+z=6 \\
& x+2 y+3 z=10 \\
& x+2 y+\lambda z=0
\end{aligned}
$$
It has unique solution.
$$
\begin{aligned}
& \therefore\left|\begin{array}{lll}
1 & 1 & 1 \\
1 & 2 & 3 \\
1 & 2 & \lambda
\end{array}\right| \neq 0 \\
& \Rightarrow \quad 1(2 \lambda-6)-1(\lambda-3)+1(2-2) \neq 0 \\
& \Rightarrow \quad 2 \lambda-6-\lambda+3 \neq 0 \Rightarrow \lambda-3 \neq 0 \Rightarrow \lambda \neq 3
\end{aligned}
$$

6λx-3y+3z=4λ2, 2x+6λy+4z=1 and 3x+2y+3λz=λ has no solution. Then,12∑λ∈Sλ is equal to _______.
question:
Let S be the set of values of λ, for which the system of equations

6λx-3y+3z=4λ2, 2x+6λy+4z=1 and 3x+2y+3λz=λ has no solution. Then,12∑λ∈Sλ is equal to _______.
solution:
Given,

System of equations,

6λx-3y+3z=4λ2

2x+6λy+4z=1

3x+2y+3λz=λ

For no solution △=0,

So, 6λ-3326λ4323λ=0

⇒9λ3-7λ-2=0

⇒(λ-1)(3λ+1)(3λ+2)=0

Hence, λ∈1,-13,-23

Now putting the value of λ in 12∑λ∈Sλ we get,

12∑λ∈Sλ=12×1+13+23=24

q:
The system of equations
$$
\begin{aligned}
& \alpha x+y+z=\alpha-1 \\
& x+\alpha y+z=\alpha-1 \\
& x+y+\alpha z=\alpha-1
\end{aligned}
$$
has no solution, if $\alpha$ is
question:
The system of equations
$$
\begin{aligned}
& \alpha x+y+z=\alpha-1 \\
& x+\alpha y+z=\alpha-1 \\
& x+y+\alpha z=\alpha-1
\end{aligned}
$$
has no solution, if $\alpha$ is
solution:
$$
\begin{aligned}
& \alpha x+y+z=\alpha-1 \\
& x+\alpha y+z=\alpha-1 \\
& x+y+z \alpha=\alpha-1 \\
& \Delta=\left|\begin{array}{ccc}
\alpha & 1 & 1 \\
1 & \alpha & 1 \\
1 & 1 & \alpha
\end{array}\right| \\
& =\alpha\left(\alpha^2-1\right)-1(\alpha-1)+1(1-\alpha) \\
& =\alpha(\alpha-1)(\alpha+1)-1(\alpha-1)-1(\alpha-1) \\
& \Rightarrow(\alpha-1)\left[\alpha^2+\alpha-1-1\right]=0 \\
& \Rightarrow(\alpha-1)\left[\alpha^2+\alpha-2\right]=0 \\
& {\left[\alpha^2+2 \alpha-\alpha-2\right]=0} \\
& (\alpha-1)[\alpha(\alpha+2)-1(\alpha+2)]=0 \\
& (\alpha-1)=0, \alpha+2=0 \Rightarrow \alpha=-2,1 ; \text { but } \alpha \neq 1
\end{aligned}
$$

subject: Mathematics
q:
If the system of linear equations
$x+2 a y+a z=0$     $x+3 b y+b z=0$ 
 $x+4 c y+c z=0$
 has a non-zero solution, then $\mathrm{a}, \mathrm{b}, \mathrm{c}$
question:
If the system of linear equations
$x+2 a y+a z=0$     $x+3 b y+b z=0$ 
 $x+4 c y+c z=0$
 has a non-zero solution, then $\mathrm{a}, \mathrm{b}, \mathrm{c}$
solution:
$\left|\begin{array}{lll}1 & 2 \mathrm{a} & \mathrm{a} \\ 1 & 3 \mathrm{~b} & \mathrm{~b} \\ 1 & 4 \mathrm{c} & \mathrm{c}\end{array}\right|=0 \quad \mathrm{C}_2 \rightarrow \mathrm{C}_2-2 \mathrm{C}_3$
$\left|\begin{array}{ccc}1 & 0 & \mathrm{a} \\ 1 & \mathrm{~b} & \mathrm{~b} \\ 1 & 2 \mathrm{c} & \mathrm{c}\end{array}\right|=0 \quad \mathrm{R}_3 \rightarrow \mathrm{R}_3-\mathrm{R}_2, \mathrm{R}_2 \rightarrow \mathrm{R}_2-\mathrm{R}_1$
$\left|\begin{array}{ccc}1 & 0 & a \\ 0 & b & b-a \\ 0 & 2 c-b & c-b\end{array}\right|=0$
$b(c-b)-(b-a)(2 c-b)=0$
On simplification,
$$
\frac{2}{\mathrm{~b}}=\frac{1}{\mathrm{a}}+\frac{1}{\mathrm{c}}
$$
$\therefore \mathrm{a}, \mathrm{b}, \mathrm{c}$ are in Harmonic Progression.

q: Let the system of equations x+2y+3z=5,2x+3y+z=9,4x+3y+λz=μ have infinite number of solutions. Then λ+2μ is equal to:
question: Let the system of equations x+2y+3z=5,2x+3y+z=9,4x+3y+λz=μ have infinite number of solutions. Then λ+2μ is equal to:
solution:
Given: x+2y+3z=5, 2x+3y+z=9 and 4x+3y+λz=μ.

For infinite solutions, Δ=Δ1=Δ2=Δ3=0.

⇒Δ=12323143λ=0

⇒3λ-3-4λ+8+18-36=0

⇒λ=−13

Now, finding 

⇒Δ1=523931μ3−13=0

⇒μ=15

Now, finding

⇒Δ2=1532914μλ

⇒Δ2=153291415−13

⇒Δ2=0

And 

⇒Δ3=12523943μ

⇒Δ3=1252394315

⇒Δ3=0

For λ=−13, μ=15 system of equation has infinite solutions.

⇒λ+2μ=17.

q:
If the system of equations
$\begin{aligned}
& x+2 y-3 z=2 \\
& 2 x+\lambda y+5 z=5 \\
& 14 x+3 y+\mu z=33
\end{aligned}$
has infinitely many solutions, then $\lambda+\mu$ is equal to :
question:
If the system of equations
$\begin{aligned}
& x+2 y-3 z=2 \\
& 2 x+\lambda y+5 z=5 \\
& 14 x+3 y+\mu z=33
\end{aligned}$
has infinitely many solutions, then $\lambda+\mu$ is equal to :
solution:
$\begin{aligned}
& \Delta=\left|\begin{array}{ccc}
1 & 2 & -3 \\
2 & \lambda & 5 \\
14 & 3 & \mu
\end{array}\right|=0 \Rightarrow \lambda \mu+42 \lambda-4 \mu+107=0 \\
& \Delta_1=\left|\begin{array}{ccc}
2 & 2 & -3 \\
5 & \lambda & 5 \\
33 & 3 & \mu
\end{array}\right|=0 \Rightarrow 2 \lambda \mu+99 \lambda-10 \mu+255=0 \\
& \Delta_2=\left|\begin{array}{ccc}
1 & 2 & -3 \\
2 & 5 & 5 \\
14 & 33 & \mu
\end{array}\right|=0 \Rightarrow \mu=13
\end{aligned}$
Also, $\lambda=-1$
Hence, $\lambda+\mu=13-1=12$

As, $1+\omega^{\mathrm{n}}+\omega^{2 \mathrm{n}}=0 ; \quad \therefore \Delta=0$

subject: Mathematics
q: If $a^2+b^2+c^2=-2$ and $f(x)=\left|\begin{array}{ccc}1+a^2 x & \left(1+b^2\right) x & \left(1+c^2\right) x \\ \left(1+a^2\right) x & 1+b^2 x & \left(1+c^2\right) x \\ \left(1+a^2\right) x & \left(1+b^2\right) x & 1+c^2 x\end{array}\right|$ then $f(x)$ is $a$ polynomial of degree
question: If $a^2+b^2+c^2=-2$ and $f(x)=\left|\begin{array}{ccc}1+a^2 x & \left(1+b^2\right) x & \left(1+c^2\right) x \\ \left(1+a^2\right) x & 1+b^2 x & \left(1+c^2\right) x \\ \left(1+a^2\right) x & \left(1+b^2\right) x & 1+c^2 x\end{array}\right|$ then $f(x)$ is $a$ polynomial of degree
solution:
$$
\begin{aligned}
& f(x)=\left|\begin{array}{ccc}
1+\left(a^2+b^2+c^2+2\right) x & \left(1+b^2\right) x & \left(1+c^2\right) x \\
1+\left(a^2+b^2+c^2+2\right) x & 1+b^2 x & \left(1+c^2\right) x \\
1+\left(a^2+b^2+c^2+2\right) x & \left(1+b^2\right) x & 1+c^2 x
\end{array}\right| \text {, Applying } C_1 \rightarrow C_1+C_2+C_3 \\
& =\left|\begin{array}{ccc}
1 & \left(1+b^2\right) x & \left(1+c^2\right) x \\
1 & 1+b^2 x & \left(1+c^2\right) x \\
1 & \left(1+b^2\right) x & 1+c^2 x
\end{array}\right| \because a^2+b^2+c^2+2=0 \\
& f(x)=\left|\begin{array}{ccc}
0 & x-1 & 0 \\
0 & 1-x & x-1 \\
1 & \left(1+b^2\right) x & 1+c x
\end{array}\right| ; \text { Applying } R_1 \rightarrow R_1-R_2, R_2 \rightarrow R_2-R_3 \\
& f(x)=(x-1)^2
\end{aligned}
$$
Hence degree $=2$.

subject: Mathematics
q:
If the system of linear equations.

8x+y+4z=-2

x+y+z=0

λx-3y=μ

has infinitely many solutions, then the distance of the point λ,μ,-12 from the plane 8x+y+4z+2=0 is:
question:
If the system of linear equations.

8x+y+4z=-2

x+y+z=0

λx-3y=μ

has infinitely many solutions, then the distance of the point λ,μ,-12 from the plane 8x+y+4z+2=0 is:
solution:
Given the system of linear equations.

8x+y+4z=-2

x+y+z=0

λx-3y=μ

has infinitely many solutions, so

D=814111λ-30=0⇒λ=4

Also D1=D2=D3=0

D1=-214011μ-30=0⇒-6-3μ=0

So μ=-2

Hence the point will be 4,-2,-12

Now distance of point 4,-2,-12 from plane 8x+y+4z+2=0

=32-2-2+282+12+42=309=103

subject: Mathematics
q:
If the following system of linear equations
2x+y+z=5
x-y+z=3
x+y+a z=b
has no solution, then :
question:
If the following system of linear equations
2x+y+z=5
x-y+z=3
x+y+a z=b
has no solution, then :
solution:
Given system of equations

2x+y+z=5
x-y+z=3
x+y+a z=b
has no solution, 

D=2111-1111a=0⇒-2a-2-a+1+2=0⇒a=13

D1=5113-11b1a=5-a-1 + -13a-b +13+b = -8a+2b-2

D2=2511311ba=23a-b -5 a-1+1b-3 = a-b+3

D3=2151-1311b=2-b-3 - 1 b-3 + 52 = 7-3b

For the system to have no solution ⇒D = 0 , D3 ≠ 0

∴ a = 13 , b ≠73.

subject: Mathematics
q:
Let the system of equations
$\begin{aligned}
& x+5 y-z=1 \\ & 4 x+3 y-3 z=7 \\ & 24 x+y+\lambda z=\mu
\end{aligned}$
$\lambda, \mu \in \mathrm{R}$, have infinitely many solutions. Then the number of the solutions of this system, If $x, y, z$ are integers and satisfy $7 \leq x+y+z \leq 77$, is
question:
Let the system of equations
$\begin{aligned}
& x+5 y-z=1 \\ & 4 x+3 y-3 z=7 \\ & 24 x+y+\lambda z=\mu
\end{aligned}$
$\lambda, \mu \in \mathrm{R}$, have infinitely many solutions. Then the number of the solutions of this system, If $x, y, z$ are integers and satisfy $7 \leq x+y+z \leq 77$, is
solution:
For infinitely many solution
$\begin{aligned}
& \Delta=0 \\ & \left|\begin{array}{ccc}
1 & 5 & -1 \\ 4 & 3 & -3 \\ 24 & 1 & \lambda
\end{array}\right|=0 \\ & \Rightarrow 1(3 \lambda+3)-5(4 \lambda+72)-1(4-72)=0 \\ & \Rightarrow-17 \lambda+3-4 \times 72-4=0 \\ & \Rightarrow 17 \lambda=-289
\end{aligned}$

$\begin{aligned} & \Delta 1=0 \\ & \Rightarrow\left|\begin{array}{ccc}1 & 5 & -1 \\ 7 & 3 & -3 \\ \mu & 1 & -17\end{array}\right|=0 \\ & \Rightarrow 1(-51+3)-5(-119+3 \mu)-1(7-3 \mu)=0 \\ & \Rightarrow-48+595-15 \mu-7+3 \mu=0 \\ & \Rightarrow 12 \mu=540\end{aligned}$
$\begin{aligned} & x+5 y-z=1 \\ & 4 x+3 y-3 z=7 \\ & 24 x+y-17 z=45 \\ & \text { Let } z=1 \\ & x+5 y=1+\lambda] \times 4 \\ & 4 x+3 y=7+3 \lambda \\ & 4 x+20 y=4+4 \lambda \\ & \frac{-17 y=3-\lambda}{-17}\end{aligned}$
$\begin{aligned} & \begin{aligned} \mathrm{y} & =\frac{\lambda-3}{17}, \mathrm{x}=1+\lambda-\frac{5 \lambda-15}{17} \\ & =\frac{32-12 \lambda}{17} \\ 7 & \leq \frac{\lambda-3}{17}+\frac{32+12 \lambda}{17}+\lambda \leq 77 \\ 7 & \leq \frac{30 \lambda+29}{17} \leq 77 \\ 3 & \leq \lambda \leq 42 \\ \lambda & =3,20,37\end{aligned}\end{aligned}$

q:
Let N denote the number that turns up when a fair die is rolled. If the probability that the system of equations

x+y+z=12x+Ny+2z=23x+3y+Nz=3

has unique solution is k6, then the sum of value of k and all possible values of N is
question:
Let N denote the number that turns up when a fair die is rolled. If the probability that the system of equations

x+y+z=12x+Ny+2z=23x+3y+Nz=3

has unique solution is k6, then the sum of value of k and all possible values of N is
solution:
Given equations are

x+y+z=12x+Ny+2z=23x+3y+Nz=3

So,

∆=1112N233N

Applying C2↔C2-C1 and C3↔C3-C1

⇒∆=1002N-2030N-3

⇒∆=N-2N-3

For unique solution ∆≠0, so

N≠2,3

Also, N∈1,2,3,4,5,6 and for unique solution N can take values 1,4,5,6.

⇒P(system has unique solution ) =46=k6

So, k=4

Therefore, required sum is

=4+1+4+5+6=20

q: If <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi><mo>,</mo><mo> </mo><mi>y</mi><mo>,</mo><mo> </mo><mi>z</mi></math> are in arithmetic progression with common difference <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>d</mi><mo>,</mo><mo> </mo><mi>x</mi><mo>≠</mo><mn>3</mn><mi>d</mi><mo>,</mo></math> and the determinant of the matrix <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced close="]" open="["><mtable><mtr><mtd><mn>3</mn></mtd><mtd><mn>4</mn><msqrt><mn>2</mn></msqrt></mtd><mtd><mi>x</mi></mtd></mtr><mtr><mtd><mn>4</mn></mtd><mtd><mn>5</mn><msqrt><mn>2</mn></msqrt></mtd><mtd><mi>y</mi></mtd></mtr><mtr><mtd><mn>5</mn></mtd><mtd><mi>k</mi></mtd><mtd><mi>z</mi></mtd></mtr></mtable></mfenced></math> is zero, then the value of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mi>k</mi><mn>2</mn></msup></math> is
question: If <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi><mo>,</mo><mo> </mo><mi>y</mi><mo>,</mo><mo> </mo><mi>z</mi></math> are in arithmetic progression with common difference <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>d</mi><mo>,</mo><mo> </mo><mi>x</mi><mo>≠</mo><mn>3</mn><mi>d</mi><mo>,</mo></math> and the determinant of the matrix <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced close="]" open="["><mtable><mtr><mtd><mn>3</mn></mtd><mtd><mn>4</mn><msqrt><mn>2</mn></msqrt></mtd><mtd><mi>x</mi></mtd></mtr><mtr><mtd><mn>4</mn></mtd><mtd><mn>5</mn><msqrt><mn>2</mn></msqrt></mtd><mtd><mi>y</mi></mtd></mtr><mtr><mtd><mn>5</mn></mtd><mtd><mi>k</mi></mtd><mtd><mi>z</mi></mtd></mtr></mtable></mfenced></math> is zero, then the value of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mi>k</mi><mn>2</mn></msup></math> is
solution:
Given x, y, z are in arithmetic progression, hence 2y=x+z, also 342x452y5kz=0

Applying R2→R1+R3-2R2

⇒342x0k-6205kz=0

⇒(k-62)(3z-5x)=0

⇒(k-62)=0 or (3z-5x)=0

If 3z-5x=0

⇒3(x+2d)-5x=0

⇒x=3d (Not possible)

⇒k=62

⇒k2=72.

q:
If $\left|\begin{array}{ccc}a-b-c & 2 a & 2 a \\ 2 b & b-c-a & 2 b \\ 2 c & 2 c & c-a-b\end{array}\right|$

$=(a+b+c)(x+a+b+c)^{2}, x \neq 0$ and $a+b+c \neq 0,$ then $x$

is equal to
question:
If $\left|\begin{array}{ccc}a-b-c & 2 a & 2 a \\ 2 b & b-c-a & 2 b \\ 2 c & 2 c & c-a-b\end{array}\right|$

$=(a+b+c)(x+a+b+c)^{2}, x \neq 0$ and $a+b+c \neq 0,$ then $x$

is equal to
solution:
$\Delta=\left|\begin{array}{ccc}a-b-c & 2 a & 2 a \\ 2 b & b-c-a & 2 b \\ 2 c & 2 c & c-a-b\end{array}\right|$

$R_{1} \rightarrow R_{1}+R_{2}+R$

$\Delta=\left|\begin{array}{ccc}a+b+c & a+b+c & a+b+c \\ 2 b & b-c-a & 2 b \\ 2 c & 2 c & c-a-b\end{array}\right|$

$=(a+b+c)\left|\begin{array}{ccc}1 & 1 & 1 \\ 2 b & b-c-a & 2 b \\ 2 c & 2 c & c-a-b\end{array}\right|$

$\begin{array}{l}

C_{1} \rightarrow C_{1}-C_{y}, C_{2} \rightarrow C_{2}-C_{3} \\

\Delta=(a+b+c)\left|\begin{array}{ccc}

0 & 0 & 1 \\

0 & -b-c-a & 2 b \\

c+a+b & c+a+b & c-a-b

\end{array}\right|

\end{array}$

$=(a+b+c)(a+b+c)^{2}$

Hence, $x=-2(a+b+c)$

subject: Mathematics
q:
If $$
\begin{aligned}
&\left.\mid \begin{array}{ccc}
a^2 & b^2 & c^2 \\
(a+\lambda)^2 & (b+\lambda)^2 & \left(c+\lambda^2\right) \\
(a-\lambda)^2 & \left(b-\lambda^2\right) & \left(-\lambda^2\right.
\end{array}\right) \\
&=k \lambda\left|\begin{array}{ccc}
a^2 & b^2 & c^2 \\
a & b & c \\
1 & 1 & 1
\end{array}\right|, \lambda \neq 0
\end{aligned}
$$
then $\mathrm{k}$ is equal to:
question:
If $$
\begin{aligned}
&\left.\mid \begin{array}{ccc}
a^2 & b^2 & c^2 \\
(a+\lambda)^2 & (b+\lambda)^2 & \left(c+\lambda^2\right) \\
(a-\lambda)^2 & \left(b-\lambda^2\right) & \left(-\lambda^2\right.
\end{array}\right) \\
&=k \lambda\left|\begin{array}{ccc}
a^2 & b^2 & c^2 \\
a & b & c \\
1 & 1 & 1
\end{array}\right|, \lambda \neq 0
\end{aligned}
$$
then $\mathrm{k}$ is equal to:
solution:
Let $\Delta=\left|\begin{array}{ccc}a^2 & b^2 & c^2 \\ (a+\lambda)^2 & (b+\lambda)^2 & (c+\lambda)^2 \\ (a-\lambda)^2 & (b-\lambda)^2 & (c-\lambda)^2\end{array}\right|$
$$
\begin{aligned}
&\text { Apply } \mathrm{R}_2 \rightarrow \mathrm{R}_2-\mathrm{R}_3 \\
&\Delta=\left|\begin{array}{ccc}
a^2 & b^2 & c^2 \\
(a+\lambda)^2-(a-\lambda)^2 & (b+\lambda)^2-(b-\lambda)^2 & (c+\lambda)^2-(c-\lambda)^2 \\
(a-\lambda)^2 & (b-\lambda)^2 & (c-\lambda)^2
\end{array}\right| \\
&=\left|\begin{array}{ccc}
a^2 & b^2 & c^2 \\
4 a \lambda & 4 b \lambda & 4 c \lambda \\
(a-\lambda)^2 & (b-\lambda)^2 & (c-\lambda)^2
\end{array}\right| \\
&\left(\because(x+y)^2-(x-y)^2=4 x y\right) \\
&
\end{aligned}
$$
Taking out 4 common from $\mathrm{R}_2$
$=4\left|\begin{array}{ccc}a^2 & b^2 & c^2 \\ a \lambda & b \lambda & c \lambda \\ a^2+\lambda^2-2 a \lambda & b^2+\lambda^2-2 b \lambda & c^2+\lambda^2-2 c \lambda\end{array}\right|$


$$
\begin{aligned}
&\text { Apply } \mathrm{R}_3 \rightarrow\left[\mathrm{R}_3-\left(\mathrm{R}_1-2 \mathrm{R}_2\right)\right] \\
&=4\left|\begin{array}{lll}
a^2 & b^2 & c^2 \\
a \lambda & b \lambda & c \lambda \\
\lambda^2 & \lambda^2 & \lambda^2
\end{array}\right|
\end{aligned}
$$
Taking out $\lambda$ common from $\mathrm{R}_2$ and $\lambda^2$ from $\mathrm{R}_3$.
$$
=4 \lambda\left(\lambda^2\right)\left|\begin{array}{ccc}
a^2 & b^2 & c^2 \\
a & b & c \\
1 & 1 & 1
\end{array}\right|
$$
$$
\begin{aligned}
&=k \lambda\left|\begin{array}{ccc}
a^2 & b^2 & c^2 \\
a & b & c \\
1 & 1 & 1
\end{array}\right| \\
&\Rightarrow k=4 \lambda^2
\end{aligned}
$$

subject: Mathematics
q: Let the numbers <math><mn>2</mn><mo>,</mo><mi> </mi><mi>b</mi><mo>,</mo><mi> </mi><mi>c</mi></math> be in an A.P. and <math><mi>A</mi><mo>=</mo><mfenced close="]" open="[" separators="|"><mrow><mtable><mtr><mtd><mn>1</mn></mtd><mtd><mn>1</mn></mtd><mtd><mn>1</mn></mtd></mtr><mtr><mtd><mn>2</mn></mtd><mtd><mi>b</mi></mtd><mtd><mi>c</mi></mtd></mtr><mtr><mtd><mn>4</mn></mtd><mtd><msup><mrow><mi>b</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup></mtd><mtd><msup><mrow><mi>c</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup></mtd></mtr></mtable></mrow></mfenced></math> . If <math><mi mathvariant="normal">d</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">e</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">t</mi><mo>(</mo><mi>A</mi><mo>)</mo><mi> </mi><mo>∈</mo><mo>[</mo><mn>2,16</mn><mo>]</mo><mo>,</mo></math> then <math><mi>c</mi></math> lies in the interval:
question: Let the numbers <math><mn>2</mn><mo>,</mo><mi> </mi><mi>b</mi><mo>,</mo><mi> </mi><mi>c</mi></math> be in an A.P. and <math><mi>A</mi><mo>=</mo><mfenced close="]" open="[" separators="|"><mrow><mtable><mtr><mtd><mn>1</mn></mtd><mtd><mn>1</mn></mtd><mtd><mn>1</mn></mtd></mtr><mtr><mtd><mn>2</mn></mtd><mtd><mi>b</mi></mtd><mtd><mi>c</mi></mtd></mtr><mtr><mtd><mn>4</mn></mtd><mtd><msup><mrow><mi>b</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup></mtd><mtd><msup><mrow><mi>c</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup></mtd></mtr></mtable></mrow></mfenced></math> . If <math><mi mathvariant="normal">d</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">e</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">t</mi><mo>(</mo><mi>A</mi><mo>)</mo><mi> </mi><mo>∈</mo><mo>[</mo><mn>2,16</mn><mo>]</mo><mo>,</mo></math> then <math><mi>c</mi></math> lies in the interval:
solution:
1112bc4b2c2→C2→C2-C1, C3→C3-C11002b-2c-24b2-4c2-4=b-2c-2c-b
Let common difference of AP=d
∴ b=2+d,   c=2+2d
A=d×2d×d=2d3∈2, 16
⇒d3∈1, 8⇒d∈1, 2⇒2d∈2, 4
⇒2+2d∈4, 6
∴ c∈4, 6

subject: Mathematics
q: The greatest value of <math><mi>c</mi><mo>∈</mo><mi>R</mi></math> for which the system of linear equations <math><mi>x</mi><mo>-</mo><mi>c</mi><mi>y</mi><mo>-</mo><mi>c</mi><mi>z</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>0</mn></math>, <math><mi>c</mi><mi>x</mi><mo>-</mo><mi>y</mi><mo>+</mo><mi>c</mi><mi>z</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>0</mn></math>, <math><mi>c</mi><mi>x</mi><mo>+</mo><mi>c</mi><mi>y</mi><mo>-</mo><mi>z</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>0</mn></math> has a non-trivial solution, is
question: The greatest value of <math><mi>c</mi><mo>∈</mo><mi>R</mi></math> for which the system of linear equations <math><mi>x</mi><mo>-</mo><mi>c</mi><mi>y</mi><mo>-</mo><mi>c</mi><mi>z</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>0</mn></math>, <math><mi>c</mi><mi>x</mi><mo>-</mo><mi>y</mi><mo>+</mo><mi>c</mi><mi>z</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>0</mn></math>, <math><mi>c</mi><mi>x</mi><mo>+</mo><mi>c</mi><mi>y</mi><mo>-</mo><mi>z</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>0</mn></math> has a non-trivial solution, is
solution:
∵ System of equation has non trivial solution. It is possible only when ∆=0.
∴∆ =1-c-cc-1ccc-1=0
⇒11-c2+c-c-c2-cc2+c=0
⇒2c3+3c2-1=0
⇒c=-1, 12 

Hence, the greatest value of c=12.
